body.title: [sprint] Intent extractor: DRAFTS and VALIDATES intentions — it never WRITES the intention field. Direction is the whole safety property.
body.intention: I intend that the intent extractor proposes and checks, but never asserts: it DRAFTS a candidate intention for a human to ratify, and it VALIDATES a declared intention against what the code actually does (flagging mismatch, never overwriting) — and its output NEVER lands in the `intention` field on its own. A human moves it there. [intention_date: 2026-07-13]
body.description:
**⚠ RE-SCOPED 2026-07-13 (Akien ruling, conflict C3). The original intention was: 'the intention field is populated by INFERENCE from existing ticket prose + affected file symbols, NOT by manual authoring.' THAT IS CUT.** Two reasons, and the second is decisive.

**(1) IT FABRICATES AT THE WORST POSSIBLE PLACE.** Akien 2026-07-13: an artifact's intention is what you pick up FIRST when you go to fix it — 'no deducing... this literally saves additional tool calls.' A FABRICATED intention sitting there is a confident wrong answer at the exact point where deduction was supposed to stop. It is STRICTLY WORSE THAN ABSENT: an absent intention costs you a deduction; a wrong one costs you a deduction AND sends you the wrong way. Measured the same day: CC read a STALE intention field on T-ticket-close-requires-proof, believed it, and reported a settled question as a live conflict. The outline's header rule already said it — 'a fabricated why is worse than a marked hole, because it doesn't announce itself.'

**(2) IT IS CIRCULAR, AND THIS KILLS IT ON PRINCIPLE — NO MODEL IS GOOD ENOUGH TO FIX IT.** The corpus already holds the rule: 'You must know the intention to know what to break. Mutation needs the property, and the property IS the intention — so intention -> mutation -> red.' An intention INFERRED FROM THE CODE is DOWNSTREAM of the thing it is supposed to constrain. Derive the property from the implementation, then test the implementation against it, and it PASSES ON ANY IMPLEMENTATION — INCLUDING A HOLLOW ONE. An intention inferred from code is not a SPEC, it is a DESCRIPTION. Proof-on-close needs a spec.

**AND THE OLD COMPLETION CRITERIA SAID THE QUIET PART:** 'produces a PLAUSIBLE intention sentence for 5 sample tickets; at least 3 match the manually-authored original.' Plausible is the hollow-build word, and a 40% fabrication rate was written down as SUCCESS — into the field everything reads first.

**⚠ CORRECTION — I GOT THIS BACKWARDS, AND THE WAY I GOT IT WRONG IS THE POINT.** I first read the 1,722 'unknown' values in `inferred_intention` as CP1 WORKING IN PRODUCTION — a device honestly refusing rather than confabulating. **IT IS NOT. IT IS A CRASH.** Queried devlab.predictions 2026-07-13: of 2,504 predictions, **2,435 are `unknown` at confidence EXACTLY 0.00 — the `except Exception` fallback path in device.py:191** — and **ZERO** are `unknown` at confidence > 0, which is what a genuine model refusal would look like. Only 69 real answers. **THE EXTRACTOR IS ~97% BROKEN AND HAS BEEN SILENTLY DEGRADING TO 'unknown' AT SCALE.** Live repro in the log when this very ticket was filed: `IntentExtractorDevice.predict: inference failed: 'list' object has no attribute 'get'` — `_parse_json` returns a LIST and `.get()` blows up; the broad `except` swallows it.

**THE DESIGN DEFECT IS WORSE THAN THE BUG.** A CRASH and an HONEST REFUSAL emit the IDENTICAL STRING. There is no way to tell them apart from the ticket field — you must query the confidence column to discriminate. This is the ossification hazard exactly ('a passing check and an absent check look identical') and it violates R-feedback-is-unconditional-silence-is-never-success: the device's failure was reported as its virtue. It fooled CC for twenty minutes ON THE DAY CC WAS WRITING THE RULE AGAINST IT. Ticketed: T-intent-extractor-crash-masquerades-as-refusal.

**WHAT SURVIVES:** ZERO of the 1,732 inferred values ever became the real `intention` field — that part holds, and it is why nothing must be reverted. But it holds by ACCIDENT (the write-back was never wired), NOT by principle. The direction-safety this ticket now mandates is therefore not a restatement of existing behaviour; it is the thing that makes the accident into a guarantee.

**THE REAL PRESSURE, MEASURED, AND IT IS LEGITIMATE:** 202 LIVE tickets carry no intention — and **150 of them are in `sprint`** (approved, queued to build). The other 1,718 missing ones are closed history (archaeology, low value). Per today's ruling (EVERY ticket has a proof, period) plus intention->mutation->red, THOSE 150 CANNOT BE PROVEN AS THEY STAND — there is no property to break. That is the 'closed PASS on three empty files' failure, pre-loaded 150 times. This is a real problem and it is why inference-backfill looks attractive. It must still be refused — but the 202 must be SOLVED, not ignored.

**THE RE-SCOPE — DIRECTION IS THE SAFETY PROPERTY.** The extractor's output NEVER lands in the `intention` field on its own. It lands in a PROPOSAL or a FLAG, and a HUMAN moves it. A draft presented for ratification and a value silently written are not the same act, even when the string is identical: one is a QUESTION, the other is a CLAIM.

  **JOB 1 — DRAFT (keep, and this is the answer to the 202).** Propose a candidate intention for a human to RATIFY. This honours Akien's standing preference (default to drafting, then review) and it is CP4: it saves him authoring 202 sentences from a blank page. HARD CONSTRAINT: an unratified draft is never written to `intention`. It sits in a proposal queue. Ratification is the human act that makes it real.
  **JOB 2 — VALIDATE (keep, and PROMOTE to the core).** Read the code, form what the intention APPEARS to be, and COMPARE it to the DECLARED one. Disagreement = a FLAG, never an overwrite. This is the SEMANTIC WARDEN, and it composes with the syntactic one: the DATE check (T-every-skill-declares-its-intention) says 'the file moved after the intention was written, so it is SUSPECT' — cheap, mechanical. This says 'the intention claims X and the code does Y' — expensive, semantic. THE DATE FLAGS; THE EXTRACTOR ADJUDICATES. Here, inference is running in the SAFE direction: code -> a question about the spec, never code -> the spec itself.
  **JOB 3 — FEED the scenario generator (unchanged, downstream).**
  **FORBIDDEN — writing the `intention` field.** Ever. On any confidence. This is the whole ticket.

**Affected files:** NOT `lab/claudecode/` — that path is RETIRED and INVALID (see CLAUDE.md, canonical-memory rule). Likely `devlab/claudecode/` + a proposal queue in `devlab/runtime/memory/`; validator may live with validity_sweep.py (homogeneity: it is another condition resolver — an intention has an implicit validity condition, 'the artifact still does what this says').
**Design rules:** CP1 (no confident nothing — 'unknown' is the honest output and the shipped code already does it); the outline header rule (a fabricated why is worse than a marked hole); intention->mutation->red (why inference cannot author a spec); draft-then-review (why DRAFTING is fine); escalation-is-spec-quality-data (a missing intention is ESCALATED to the human, never fabricated); homogeneity-over-special-case (reuse validity_sweep, do not birth an engine).
**Scope boundary:** IN — the draft-proposal queue with mandatory human ratification; the code-vs-declared validator emitting FLAGS; wiring the flags into the existing sweep. OUT — any auto-write to `intention` (forbidden, not deferred); acting on the intention (scenario-generator); backfilling the 1,718 closed tickets (archaeology).
**Test plan:** red->green. THE DISCRIMINATING FIXTURE — a hollow build cannot pass it: give the extractor a ticket whose DECLARED intention CONTRADICTS its code, and assert (a) the `intention` field is UNCHANGED and (b) a mismatch FLAG is raised. A build that merely 'produces a plausible sentence' fails this outright, which is exactly what the old criteria could not detect. Second fixture: a drafted candidate that is never ratified must NEVER appear in `intention` — assert the field is still empty after a full draft pass.
**Completion criteria:** (1) `intention` is never written by machine — proven by the unratified-draft fixture. (2) A contradicting intention raises a flag and overwrites nothing. (3) Drafts land in a ratification queue a human acts on. (4) The 150 sprint tickets with no intention are SURFACED as a worklist (surfacing is in scope; authoring them is Akien's, one review at a time).

--- ORIGINAL TICKET TEXT (premise CUT 2026-07-13; use case 2 survives and is now the core) ---

We have IBD: every ticket has an `intention:` field (I intend that...), manually authored at filing time. An intent extractor agent would infer this from the ticket description, affected files, and symbol map — making it a compiled artifact rather than a hand-filled field.

Use cases:
- Backfill `intention:` on tickets filed without one
- Validate that the manually-authored intention matches what the code actually does post-sprint
- Feed into scenario generator (intent → scenarios)

Observed in working form at a parallel project (test automation context, serves non-engineer test authors).

**Affected files:** TBD — new inference agent; likely lab/claudecode/ or devices/hubert/
**Design rules:** none — inference agent, no DB writes required beyond updating the ticket field
**Scope boundary:** IN — infer intention from prose + symbols. OUT — acting on the intention (that's scenario-generator).
**Completion criteria:** Intent extractor produces a plausible `intention:` sentence for 5 sample tickets; at least 3 match the manually-authored original within one Akien review.
body.completion_criteria: Machine never writes `intention` (proven by an unratified-draft fixture). A contradicting declared intention raises a FLAG and overwrites nothing (the discriminating fixture — a 'plausible sentence' build fails it). Drafts land in a human ratification queue. The 150 live sprint tickets lacking an intention are surfaced as a worklist.
